SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper delivers a substantial new corpus, a novel crowdsourcing scheme, and a pipeline with baseline and evaluation metrics, which supports the main claims at a proof-of-concept level. However, experimental depth and reproducibility are limited: hyperparameters and implementation details (code release not mentioned) are missing, and there are few reported ablations or sensitivity analyses quantifying key choices (e.g., OpenIE filtering thresholds, TrueSkill sampling strategy, classifier settings). Related work coverage and discussion of limitations are adequate, but additional controlled comparisons, statistical rigor, and full reproducibility artefacts are needed to raise confidence further. | B(3): The paper presents a novel corpus, a clear pipeline, and an interesting crowdsourcing protocol, with baseline systems and multiple evaluation metrics—so experiments are broadly adequate to support the main claims about feasibility. However, important analyses are missing: there are no systematic ablations or sensitivity studies (e.g., effect of OpenIE quality, TrueSkill sampling parameters, or alternative graph assembly heuristics), hyperparameters and code release are not reported, and the baseline’s failure modes (especially relation extraction errors) propagate but are not deeply analyzed. Related work coverage is noted as adequate and limitations are discussed, but reproducibility and deeper experimental probing are insufficient for stronger confidence in broad applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper delivers a substantial new corpus, a novel crowdsourcing scheme, and a pipeline with baseline and evaluation metrics, which supports the main claims at a proof-of-concept level. However, experimental depth and reproducibility are limited: hyperparameters and implementation details (code release not mentioned) are missing, and there are few reported ablations or sensitivity analyses quantifying key choices (e.g., OpenIE filtering thresholds, TrueSkill sampling strategy, classifier settings). Related work coverage and discussion of limitations are adequate, but additional controlled comparisons, statistical rigor, and full reproducibility artefacts are needed to raise confidence further.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a novel corpus, a clear pipeline, and an interesting crowdsourcing protocol, with baseline systems and multiple evaluation metrics—so experiments are broadly adequate to support the main claims about feasibility. However, important analyses are missing: there are no systematic ablations or sensitivity studies (e.g., effect of OpenIE quality, TrueSkill sampling parameters, or alternative graph assembly heuristics), hyperparameters and code release are not reported, and the baseline’s failure modes (especially relation extraction errors) propagate but are not deeply analyzed. Related work coverage is noted as adequate and limitations are discussed, but reproducibility and deeper experimental probing are insufficient for stronger confidence in broad applicability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it introduces a novel dataset and a practical pipeline for multi-document summarization via concept maps, a crowdsourcing method (low-context importance annotation), and baseline and evaluation protocols aligned with community standards (strict/METEOR/ROUGE metrics). From a reproducibility standpoint I have concerns that the authors do not report implementation/hyperparameter details or clearly state code release, and their pipeline exhibits substantial extraction/relation errors (only ~76% coverage and ~17% top-25 concept recall) that warrant deeper ablations and robustness analyses. These issues are solvable and do not undermine topical relevance, so the paper deserves acceptance conditional on stronger experimental details and reproducibility artifacts. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles a core multi-document summarization task with a novel, scalable annotation protocol (low-context importance annotation) and releases a new corpus of human-authored concept-map summaries grounded in realistic DIP web clusters. The methodology (OpenIE extraction, crowdsourced importance ranking with TrueSkill, expert revision) and baseline system follow community expectations, and the evaluation uses suitable automatic metrics (strict/proposition and METEOR-style matching, ROUGE) while clearly reporting limitations (coverage and relation-extraction weakness). Given its clear relevance to summarization, structured-content generation, and human-centered evaluation, the paper addresses the right audience and is positioned well within the venue’s research community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it introduces a novel dataset and a practical pipeline for multi-document summarization via concept maps, a crowdsourcing method (low-context importance annotation), and baseline and evaluation protocols aligned with community standards (strict/METEOR/ROUGE metrics). From a reproducibility standpoint I have concerns that the authors do not report implementation/hyperparameter details or clearly state code release, and their pipeline exhibits substantial extraction/relation errors (only ~76% coverage and ~17% top-25 concept recall) that warrant deeper ablations and robustness analyses. These issues are solvable and do not undermine topical relevance, so the paper deserves acceptance conditional on stronger experimental details and reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles a core multi-document summarization task with a novel, scalable annotation protocol (low-context importance annotation) and releases a new corpus of human-authored concept-map summaries grounded in realistic DIP web clusters. The methodology (OpenIE extraction, crowdsourced importance ranking with TrueSkill, expert revision) and baseline system follow community expectations, and the evaluation uses suitable automatic metrics (strict/proposition and METEOR-style matching, ROUGE) while clearly reporting limitations (coverage and relation-extraction weakness). Given its clear relevance to summarization, structured-content generation, and human-centered evaluation, the paper addresses the right audience and is positioned well within the venue’s research community.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important and under-resourced problem—summarizing large heterogeneous document clusters as structured concept maps—and contributes a new human-authored corpus and a scalable crowdsourcing method (low-context importance annotation with TrueSkill) that practitioners can reuse. The pipeline and reported baseline expose clear bottlenecks (content selection and relation extraction) and provide concrete empirical evidence and evaluation protocols that are likely to change how researchers approach large-cluster content selection and benchmarking. While extraction and relation errors limit out-of-the-box performance, the dataset and methodology open significant follow-on research directions and are likely to be adopted by the community. | B(4): This work tackles an important and under-served problem—scaling structured summarization (concept maps) to large, heterogeneous web document clusters—and supplies an actionable resource: a 30-topic gold corpus plus a reproducible pipeline and a novel low-context importance annotation protocol (Likert + pairwise TrueSkill). The crowdsourcing scheme and released baseline lower the barrier for follow-up work on content selection and human-centered representations, and the paper candidly reports practical limitations (only ~17% top-25 coverage of gold concepts, weak relation extraction), which both highlight research gaps and make the dataset ecologically valid for downstream learning/sensemaking studies. While not yet transformative because extraction and selection remain challenging, the contribution is likely to be widely used and to open substantive new research directions in structured multi-document summarization and evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important and under-resourced problem—summarizing large heterogeneous document clusters as structured concept maps—and contributes a new human-authored corpus and a scalable crowdsourcing method (low-context importance annotation with TrueSkill) that practitioners can reuse. The pipeline and reported baseline expose clear bottlenecks (content selection and relation extraction) and provide concrete empirical evidence and evaluation protocols that are likely to change how researchers approach large-cluster content selection and benchmarking. While extraction and relation errors limit out-of-the-box performance, the dataset and methodology open significant follow-on research directions and are likely to be adopted by the community.

reviewer_b.justification: This work tackles an important and under-served problem—scaling structured summarization (concept maps) to large, heterogeneous web document clusters—and supplies an actionable resource: a 30-topic gold corpus plus a reproducible pipeline and a novel low-context importance annotation protocol (Likert + pairwise TrueSkill). The crowdsourcing scheme and released baseline lower the barrier for follow-up work on content selection and human-centered representations, and the paper candidly reports practical limitations (only ~17% top-25 coverage of gold concepts, weak relation extraction), which both highlight research gaps and make the dataset ecologically valid for downstream learning/sensemaking studies. While not yet transformative because extraction and selection remain challenging, the contribution is likely to be widely used and to open substantive new research directions in structured multi-document summarization and evaluation.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a sensible internal baseline and evaluates on the released corpus using multiple metrics (strict match, METEOR, ROUGE-2), so comparisons are carried out on the same data and under consistent conditions. However, it appears to lack comparisons to stronger external or recent competing methods for multi-document summarization / concept-map construction (beyond their heuristic baseline), which limits conclusions about practical gains. The authors do analyze error sources (content selection and relation extraction) and acknowledge limitations, and related work is discussed, but the absence of head-to-head evaluations against established baselines reduces the thoroughness of the comparative assessment. | B(3): The paper evaluates a reasonable in-house baseline (NP extraction + merging + relation extraction + Random Forest importance classifier) on the newly released 30-topic corpus using consistent metrics (strict and METEOR proposition matching, ROUGE-2), so comparisons are conducted under the same data and evaluation conditions. However, the experimental comparison appears limited to the authors’ baseline rather than established recent systems for structured/concept-graph summarization or strong multi-document summarizers, so important competitive baselines are missing. The manuscript does acknowledge when components (extraction, relation extraction) underperform, but it stops short of demonstrating where prior methods would perform comparably or better in practice. From my perspective as a scholar focused on downstream utility, the comparisons are useful but not comprehensive enough to establish clear empirical advances.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a sensible internal baseline and evaluates on the released corpus using multiple metrics (strict match, METEOR, ROUGE-2), so comparisons are carried out on the same data and under consistent conditions. However, it appears to lack comparisons to stronger external or recent competing methods for multi-document summarization / concept-map construction (beyond their heuristic baseline), which limits conclusions about practical gains. The authors do analyze error sources (content selection and relation extraction) and acknowledge limitations, and related work is discussed, but the absence of head-to-head evaluations against established baselines reduces the thoroughness of the comparative assessment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates a reasonable in-house baseline (NP extraction + merging + relation extraction + Random Forest importance classifier) on the newly released 30-topic corpus using consistent metrics (strict and METEOR proposition matching, ROUGE-2), so comparisons are conducted under the same data and evaluation conditions. However, the experimental comparison appears limited to the authors’ baseline rather than established recent systems for structured/concept-graph summarization or strong multi-document summarizers, so important competitive baselines are missing. The manuscript does acknowledge when components (extraction, relation extraction) underperform, but it stops short of demonstrating where prior methods would perform comparably or better in practice. From my perspective as a scholar focused on downstream utility, the comparisons are useful but not comprehensive enough to establish clear empirical advances.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence: a new 30-topic corpus, a detailed pipeline (Open IE extraction, confidence filtering, crowdsourced low-context importance annotation with TrueSkill), and baseline evaluations showing extraction covers ~76% of gold concepts but top-25 selection only ~17% and poor relation extraction. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and multiple evaluation metrics, but reproducibility is hindered by missing implementation details (hyperparameters, data splits, and code release) and limited statistical analysis of results. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and while the arguments are plausible, some claims (e.g., reliability of the crowdsourcing scheme and effectiveness across domains) would benefit from more calibrated inter-annotator agreement statistics and ablation studies. Overall the technical contribution is well-supported but minor gaps in experimental detail and statistical rigor prevent a flawless assessment. | B(4): The paper’s central technical claims — release of a corpus of human-authored concept-map summaries and a crowdsourcing pipeline (low-context importance annotation) for ranking propositions — are supported by concrete methodology and empirical evaluation on 30 topics drawn from DIP and a TAC2008 pilot. The experimental design includes sensible baselines, multiple evaluation metrics (strict and METEOR-based proposition matching, ROUGE-2), and candid analysis of failure modes (notably low relation-extraction quality and limited top-25 content coverage), which supports the authors’ conclusions. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and while relation-extraction weaknesses and modest content-selection recall represent limitations, these are identified and analyzed rather than ignored, so the technical claims remain largely validated. Overall the work is technically sound with minor shortcomings that do not undermine its main contributions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence: a new 30-topic corpus, a detailed pipeline (Open IE extraction, confidence filtering, crowdsourced low-context importance annotation with TrueSkill), and baseline evaluations showing extraction covers ~76% of gold concepts but top-25 selection only ~17% and poor relation extraction. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and multiple evaluation metrics, but reproducibility is hindered by missing implementation details (hyperparameters, data splits, and code release) and limited statistical analysis of results. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and while the arguments are plausible, some claims (e.g., reliability of the crowdsourcing scheme and effectiveness across domains) would benefit from more calibrated inter-annotator agreement statistics and ablation studies. Overall the technical contribution is well-supported but minor gaps in experimental detail and statistical rigor prevent a flawless assessment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central technical claims — release of a corpus of human-authored concept-map summaries and a crowdsourcing pipeline (low-context importance annotation) for ranking propositions — are supported by concrete methodology and empirical evaluation on 30 topics drawn from DIP and a TAC2008 pilot. The experimental design includes sensible baselines, multiple evaluation metrics (strict and METEOR-based proposition matching, ROUGE-2), and candid analysis of failure modes (notably low relation-extraction quality and limited top-25 content coverage), which supports the authors’ conclusions. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and while relation-extraction weaknesses and modest content-selection recall represent limitations, these are identified and analyzed rather than ignored, so the technical claims remain largely validated. Overall the work is technically sound with minor shortcomings that do not undermine its main contributions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are clearly stated early (new corpus, crowdsourcing scheme, pipeline and baseline). Writing quality is reported as good and the flow from methodology through experiments to conclusions appears logical. A few presentation omissions reduce clarity for reproducibility-oriented readers: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the summary does not state whether notation and figures/tables fully support the described pipeline. These are minor presentation issues rather than pervasive clarity problems. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized with a logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (new corpus, crowdsourcing scheme, and pipeline) are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and the methodology pipeline is explained step-by-step, with informative figures/tables and examples supporting understanding. Writing quality is good with few grammatical issues, though some evaluation details (e.g., metric motivations and limitations discussion) could be expanded for added clarity. From a reader-centred perspective the presentation is strong but has minor areas where additional detail would improve reproducibility and interpretation.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are clearly stated early (new corpus, crowdsourcing scheme, pipeline and baseline). Writing quality is reported as good and the flow from methodology through experiments to conclusions appears logical. A few presentation omissions reduce clarity for reproducibility-oriented readers: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the summary does not state whether notation and figures/tables fully support the described pipeline. These are minor presentation issues rather than pervasive clarity problems.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized with a logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (new corpus, crowdsourcing scheme, and pipeline) are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and the methodology pipeline is explained step-by-step, with informative figures/tables and examples supporting understanding. Writing quality is good with few grammatical issues, though some evaluation details (e.g., metric motivations and limitations discussion) could be expanded for added clarity. From a reader-centred perspective the presentation is strong but has minor areas where additional detail would improve reproducibility and interpretation.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper makes a notable novel contribution by releasing a new, human-authored corpus of concept-map summaries for large heterogeneous web-document clusters and by introducing a new crowdsourcing scheme (low-context importance annotation combined with sampled pairwise comparisons and TrueSkill) tailored to large-scale content selection. These elements go beyond standard multi-document summarization datasets and annotation protocols and are clearly differentiated from prior work in the summary. However, the core NLP techniques (OpenIE extraction, relation extraction, heuristic graph assembly) are largely composed of existing methods, and the authors themselves report substantial pipeline limitations, so the contribution is more notable for its resources and annotation methodology than for a fundamentally new modelling paradigm. | B(4): The paper makes a notable contribution by releasing a new corpus of human-authored summary concept maps for large, heterogeneous web-document clusters — a resource that fills a clear gap for structured, multi-document summaries. The proposed low-context importance annotation workflow (Likert + sampled pairwise comparisons with TrueSkill) appears to be an original, practical scheme tailored to scale and reliability for large clusters, and the end-to-end pipeline (OpenIE extraction → crowdsourced importance ranking → expert revision) is a non-trivial, well-differentiated assembly of methods even if it builds on existing components. Given these points, the work is more than incremental but not a radical theoretical departure, so it is appropriately judged as notably novel (4).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes a notable novel contribution by releasing a new, human-authored corpus of concept-map summaries for large heterogeneous web-document clusters and by introducing a new crowdsourcing scheme (low-context importance annotation combined with sampled pairwise comparisons and TrueSkill) tailored to large-scale content selection. These elements go beyond standard multi-document summarization datasets and annotation protocols and are clearly differentiated from prior work in the summary. However, the core NLP techniques (OpenIE extraction, relation extraction, heuristic graph assembly) are largely composed of existing methods, and the authors themselves report substantial pipeline limitations, so the contribution is more notable for its resources and annotation methodology than for a fundamentally new modelling paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes a notable contribution by releasing a new corpus of human-authored summary concept maps for large, heterogeneous web-document clusters — a resource that fills a clear gap for structured, multi-document summaries. The proposed low-context importance annotation workflow (Likert + sampled pairwise comparisons with TrueSkill) appears to be an original, practical scheme tailored to scale and reliability for large clusters, and the end-to-end pipeline (OpenIE extraction → crowdsourced importance ranking → expert revision) is a non-trivial, well-differentiated assembly of methods even if it builds on existing components. Given these points, the work is more than incremental but not a radical theoretical departure, so it is appropriately judged as notably novel (4).

main_claims: They present a newly created corpus of human-authored summary concept maps that summarize large heterogeneous web document clusters on educational topics.

They introduce a novel crowdsourcing scheme, low-context importance annotation (Likert tasks + sampled pairwise comparisons with TrueSkill), to efficiently and reliably determine important propositions in large document collections.

They provide a pipeline (Open IE extraction, confidence filtering, crowdsourced importance ranking, expert revision) to construct gold-standard concept-map summaries and release a baseline method and evaluation protocol for the task.
_summary.methodology: They automatically extract propositions from relevant sentences using Open IE, filter by extraction confidence, collect crowdsourced importance annotations (5 Likert scores then sampled pairwise comparisons with TrueSkill) to rank propositions, have an expert revise top propositions, and assemble summary concept maps; a baseline system uses NP extraction, merging, relation extraction, a Random Forest classifier for concept importance, and heuristic graph pruning.
datasets: DIP corpus (used as source data, selected 30 topics)
The newly created concept map corpus of 30 topics (released by the authors)
baselines: Baseline method: NP extraction + merging + relation extraction + Random Forest classifier for important concepts + heuristic to produce a connected 25-concept graph
limitations: Content selection is a major challenge for large clusters: extraction covers ~76% of gold concepts but top-25 selection only contains ~17%.
Relation extraction performs poorly, yielding low strict proposition matching and propagating errors through the pipeline.
The annotation task is subjective (though authors report measures showing acceptable reliability).
_summary.topic_area: Summarization - multi-document summarization / concept map generation

reviewer_a.name: Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with a track record of building and evaluating large-scale annotation pipelines and benchmarks for summarization and information extraction (industry and academic collaborations).
traits: notices missing or under-specified implementation and experimental details (hyperparameters, data splits, pre/post-processing) and values full reproducibility
requires clear baselines, ablation studies, and quantitative evidence for each claimed contribution
checks annotation quality closely (inter-annotator agreement, worker recruitment, calibration) and looks for potential biases introduced by crowdsourcing designs
values statistical rigor (confidence intervals, significance testing where appropriate) and robustness checks (sensitivity to thresholds, sampling variability)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor, practicality of the pipeline, and whether the results are reproducible and well-supported by controlled comparisons.

reviewer_b.name: Summarization & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching multi-document summarization, content representation (e.g., concept maps), and human-centered evaluation of summarization tools, with experience creating and using corpora for downstream educational applications.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior work on structured summaries, concept-graph generation, and corpus creation, and cares about where the contribution advances the field
assesses ecological validity and downstream utility (does the dataset and method reflect real user needs and tasks, e.g., learning or sensemaking?)
looks for clarity about limitations and scope (what kinds of collections and topics the approach suits) and the likely reuse value of the released dataset and protocol
interested in human-centered evaluation and whether the proposed representations align with how people read and synthesize large, heterogeneous document clusters
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Places emphasis on novelty, broader significance, and potential impact of the dataset and method on the research community and real-world use cases.
